exemplar.title: An Investigation into Reconstructive Memory Theory

detailedExemplarData.title: An Investigation into Reconstructive Memory Theory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused aim with explained relevance to reconstructive memory theory
Accurate description of schema theory and the multiâstore model with explicit linkage to the investigation
Wellâoperationalised hypotheses with IV and DV clearly defined in units
weaknesses: Some repetition in phrasing obscures clarity (e.g., redundant mention of memory reconstruction)
Connection of the multiâstore model mechanisms to the hypothesis is not fully developed
Text cuts off midâsentence in one place, leaving conditions incompletely defined
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently meets topâband requirements: aim and relevance are clearly stated and explained, theory is described and linked, and variables are operationalised in the hypothesis. Minor lapses in clarity and depth warrant a highâband score of 5 rather than a perfect 6.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is not only described but the rationale (avoiding order effects) is explained
Convenience sampling is described and critiqued in terms of bias and representativeness
Key controlled variables and materials are identified with some justification for consistency
weaknesses: Explanation of controlled variables/materials lacks depthâchoice of verb extremes (âsmashedâ vs. âcontactedâ) is under-justified
Discussion of how sampling bias specifically impacts inferential statistics is superficial
Operational details of materials (e.g., formal selection criteria for outliers) are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While research design and sampling are explained at a high level, the variables and materials strand remains only partially developed. Two strands in the top band and one in the mid band justify an overall exploration score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate choice of descriptive (median, IQR) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics
Clear graphical presentation with a correctly labelled bar chart addressing the hypothesis
Findings are linked to hypothesis, with U, z and p reported and interpreted
weaknesses: Calculation and critical-value reporting errors (e.g., U = 20.5 vs. critical value of 5) reduce accuracy
Data level is mis-labelled in places, and dispersion could be better visualised (no IQR on graph)
Outlier handling is subjective without formal criteria (e.g., IQR rule)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies both descriptive and inferential methods but makes notable calculation and reporting errors. Graphical presentation and interpretation are strong, yet the statistical inaccuracies limit the criterion to the mid band (3 out of 6).

strengths: Strengths and limitations are stated, described and directly linked to validity and generalisability
Modifications are explicitly justified in relation to identified limitations
Findings are related back to schema theory, showing theoretical engagement
weaknesses: Discussion of findings lacks depthâalternative explanations or wider literature are not explored
Ethical considerations around participant removal are not addressed
Conclusion makes some overgeneralised claims without quantitative reinforcement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (strengths/limitations and modifications) fully meet the top band, while the findings discussion remains only partially developed. This balance of strong evaluative insight with some gaps merits a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart displaying median speed for each verb is included, labelled and directly addresses the hypothesis.  Axes and values appear correct; there are no visible graphing errors.  The presentation therefore meets the highest descriptor, though sophistication is limited, so a 5 rather than 6 is awarded.

The student states statistical results (median differences; U, z, p) and clearly links them to rejecting the null and supporting the research hypothesis.  Interpretation is connected to the data pattern.  Minor inaccuracy about critical value wording reduces precision slightly, so a 5 within the top band is appropriate.

Both research and null hypotheses are stated; the IV (verb with two levels: âsmashedâ v. âcontactedâ) and DV (estimated speed in km/h) are explicitly identified and operationalised.  The operationalisation includes unit choice, wording of the critical question and description of conditions, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

Sampling technique (convenience) is described and then critiqued in terms of representativeness and bias; participant characteristics (age, language, driving experience) are considered, showing an explanatory stance rather than a simple list.  This meets the âexplainedâ requirement for the top band in Exploration.

Schema theory and reconstructive memory are described with reference to Bartlett, Roediger and the MSM, showing accurate knowledge.  The narrative then explicitly connects these concepts to the present investigation (e.g., âschema can allow participants to pierce fragments â¦ reconstructing their memory of the event, which is the crash speedâ).  The linkage is clear and accurate, though not exhaustively analysed, meriting high-band credit.

Findings are related back to reconstructive memory and schema theory, but the discussion is brief and lacks depth; competing explanations or wider literature are not explored.  This satisfies âdescribed with referenceâ but not the fuller âdiscussedâ requirement for 5-6.

The student states a clear aim â to investigate how the verbs âsmashedâ versus âcontactedâ influence speed estimates â and explicitly links this to the broader question of how post-event information alters memory.  In several sentences they justify why understanding reconstructive memory is important (âhow susceptible human minds are to alterationsâ and the role of schema).  The relevance is therefore more than merely identified; it is explained, though not in extensive depth, so a solid but not perfect score within the 5-6 band is warranted.

Multiple strengths (independent measures, control of extraneous variables) and limitations (convenience sampling, demand characteristics) are identified and explained in relation to validity and generalisability.  The analysis is relevant and goes beyond listing, so the high band is warranted, though explanation could be slightly more nuanced for full marks.

Key controlled variables (same video, volume, scripted instructions) and materials (video source, answer sheet with units) are identified and some justification is provided for the verb choices.  While an explanation is offered, it is less comprehensive than for design and sampling, so a mid-to-upper score in the 3-4 band is appropriate.

Proposed modifications (older participants, matched-pairs design, procedural controls) are explicitly linked to named limitations (sample bias, driver experience, order effects) and justified in terms of improving reliability or validity.  The justification is clear but not exhaustive, supporting a strong score within the top band.

The student labels the design as independent measures and explains the rationale (avoiding order effects and demand characteristics).  This moves beyond mere description to explanation, fulfilling the 3-4 band, and the account is generally accurate, so the upper mark in that band is appropriate.

Both descriptive and inferential statistics are applied and broadly appropriate (median/IQR and Mann-Whitney U).  However, there are calculation/reporting errors: the text claims U = 20.5 is below a critical value of 5, and mis-labels data level (ânominal and ratioâ).  These mistakes prevent placement in the 5-6 band, but correct methods are attempted, so 3-4 band with a mid score is justified.

annotations.comment: Suggestion: Add interquartile range indicators on the graph to visually convey dispersion differences rather than describing them solely in text.
Weakness: The interpretation links dispersion to severity but overextends by inferring schema processes; focus interpretation on statistical meaning before theoretical implications.
Strength: The data table succinctly summarises medians and IQRs for both conditions, aiding clarity in statistical reporting.
Weakness: The report incorrectly states that U = 20.50 is below the critical value of 5; confirm the correct critical value and ensure p-value interpretation aligns with standard thresholds.
Weakness: The evaluation briefly restates findings without connecting to alternative theories or considering external validity; deepen discussion by exploring contrasting explanations.
Strength: The title clearly identifies the focus on reconstructive memory theory, providing a precise framing of the investigation.

The description of schema theory accurately references Bartlett (1932) but could be tightened by explicitly linking the schema construct to participantsâ speed estimates rather than broadly to past experiences.

Weakness: The integration of the Multi-store Memory Model is mentioned but the specific mechanisms by which encoding and retrieval influence post-event information processing are not clearly connected to the hypothesis.
Strength: The student succinctly describes Loftus and Palmerâs use of verb manipulation, clearly differentiating âsmashedâ versus âcontactedâ conditions and their semantic implications.
Suggestion: The text cuts off mid-sentence when introducing the two verb conditions; ensure complete definitions of all conditions to avoid ambiguity.
Strength: The operationalisation of the dependent variable (estimated speed in km/h) is clearly defined.

Weakness: The sentence âThis study aims to investigate the reconstruction of memory as a result of the reconstruction of memoryâ is repetitive and obscures the actual aim; consider rephrasing to clearly state the research objective.
Strength: Both one-tailed research and null hypotheses are explicitly stated with clear operationalisation of IV and DV, satisfying top-band requirements.

Weakness: The convenience sampling description notes lack of randomisation but could deepen the explanation by discussing how selection bias might specifically impact the validity of inferential statistics.
Strength: Key control variables (consistent video, volume, script) are clearly identified, demonstrating attention to internal validity.

Weakness: The rationale for verb choice is based on understandability but lacks justification regarding severity scale equivalence; consider explaining why âsmashedâ and âcontactedâ represent the extremes.
Strength: The student correctly identifies the data as ratio level and justifies using the median, demonstrating appropriate descriptive statistic selection.
Weakness: The criteria for outlier identification (âtoo bigâ or âtoo slowâ) are subjective; consider applying a formal definition (e.g., IQR rule) to support outlier classification.
Strength: The bar chart is clearly labelled, uses appropriate scales, and directly addresses the hypothesis by comparing medians of the two conditions.
Strength: The student thoroughly explains how convenience sampling leads to selection bias and limited generalisability, demonstrating reflective evaluation.
Suggestion: The matched-pairs design proposal is strong but could be justified with reference to its statistical benefits, such as reduced variance.

Weakness: While demand characteristics are acknowledged, the handling of negative-participant role by removing data points needs ethical reflection; discuss potential impacts on validity and data integrity.
Suggestion: Introduce a clear statement linking the overall findings back to the investigation aim and theoretical framework to strengthen coherence.
Weakness: The conclusion asserts unsupported claims about reconstructive memory efficacy; include quantitative summary of results to reinforce claims and avoid overgeneralising.
